names.name: carving an expression of political loyalty

description.div: While underwater quarrying of coral rocks was a common activity on Penghu, the quarrying of basalt stone under water was practiced in few areas only, due to the variation on Penghu of the quality of basalt stones, and very likely restricted through the accessibility of the sea floor in terms of sea currents and the deapth of the water. The advantages of underwater quarry on Penghu were twofold. First, the sea and the sea floor were treated as commons and the stone was thus freely available. No land need to be acquired. This offered especially relatively poor people to engage in this business. Second, there is the more general advantage that moving the heavy stone, with a density near three, was more easy, in terms of weight, in water than on land due to the buoyancy of the ocean water. Nevertheless underwater quarrying seems a remarkable and dangereous practice that was maybe practiced only by those families that could not afford to buy the necessary land.

There are traces of working the basalt stone on Gupoyu, as well as on the northern coast line of Baisha. Most likely, the second group of specialists was working along the shores to bring the stones into the required form, e.g. the form of a tombstone, before shipping the object to the third group of specialists, the tombstone carvers. This eventually would save energy in the handling and transportation of the rocks and stones. The existance of these three groups of stone-working specialists has been mentioned by Mister WU, the carver from Jiangmei on Baisha.

Despite this very convincing work flow, inscribed tombstones are found on Gupoyu Island. The most plausible explanation might be that the tombstone carvers eventually also worked on Gupoyu in order to avoid unnecessarily complex and burdensome transportation, e.g. to another island than Baisha where the carvers mainly concentrated.
